meta.title: Social Medicine & Public Health — Recalled MCQs

behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Vaccination is which type of prevention?
questions.explanation: Vaccination prevents disease onset in healthy people → primary prevention. (Screening = secondary.)

questions.stem: What does "70% sensitivity" of a diagnostic/screening test mean?

options.A: 70% of people who test positive truly have the disease
options.B: The test correctly identifies 70% of people who truly have the disease
options.D: 70% of the population is at risk

questions.explanation: Sensitivity = a/(a+c): of everyone who truly has the disease, 70% are caught (30% are false negatives). Option A describes PPV; option C describes specificity.

questions.stem: Negative predictive value (NPV) is:

options.A: The probability disease is present given a positive test
options.B: The probability disease is absent given a negative test
options.C: The proportion of diseased who test negative
options.D: The proportion of healthy who test positive

questions.explanation: NPV = d/(c+d) = given a negative result, the probability the person is truly disease-free. It rises when prevalence is low.

questions.stem: PSA testing with low specificity leads chiefly to:
questions.explanation: PSA is highly sensitive but poorly specific (~20%); combined with indolent tumours this causes overdiagnosis and overtreatment.

questions.stem: According to Rogers' innovation curve, the group that stubbornly resists change is:
questions.explanation: Laggards ≈ 16% resist and may never adopt. Full population adoption is not required for improvement.

questions.stem: What goes on the head of a fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram?
questions.explanation: The head states the problem/effect; the "bones" are categories of contributing causes. It is a tool for understanding the system.

questions.stem: "STEEEPI" (the 7 dimensions of quality of care) stands for:

options.A: Safe, Timely, Efficient, Effective, Equitable, People-centred, Integrated
options.B: Screening, Treatment, Education, Ethics, Prevention, Policy, Integration
options.C: Sensitivity, Timeliness, Equity, Efficacy, Economy, Prevention, Information
options.D: Structure, Timing, Evaluation, Evidence, Practice, Process, Impact

questions.explanation: IOM dimensions: Safe, Timely, Efficient, Effective, Equitable, People-centred, Integrated.

questions.stem: The correct order of the public-health surveillance cycle is:
questions.explanation: Surveillance = ongoing Collection → Analysis → Interpretation → Dissemination (→ link to action).

questions.stem: The first step in an outbreak investigation is to:

options.B: Confirm the outbreak exists and verify the diagnosis

questions.explanation: You first confirm the outbreak & verify the diagnosis; a case definition and case counting follow before hypothesis testing and control.

questions.stem: In an outbreak investigation, a "case definition" is:

options.B: A standard set of criteria (clinical + time/place/person) to decide who counts as a case

questions.explanation: A case definition is standard criteria that make case counting consistent across investigators.

questions.stem: CERC (Crisis & Emergency Risk Communication) principles include:

options.A: Be First, Be Right, Be Credible, Empathy, Promote Action, Respect

questions.explanation: CDC's CERC: Be First, Be Right, Be Credible, Express Empathy, Promote Action, Show Respect.

questions.stem: One Health is best described as:

options.A: Free universal healthcare for all humans
options.B: An integrated approach linking human, animal, plant and ecosystem health
options.C: A single global health insurance scheme
options.D: Treating one disease at a time

questions.explanation: One Health = integrated, multisectoral approach recognising interdependence of human–animal–plant–environment health (zoonoses, AMR, food safety). Quadripartite: WHO+FAO+WOAH+UNEP.

questions.stem: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) results mainly from:
questions.explanation: AMR arises from the misuse & overuse of antimicrobials — a flagship One Health problem.

questions.stem: How many Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are in the 2030 Agenda, and which one is health?
questions.explanation: 17 SDGs (169 targets), adopted 2015 by 193 states; SDG 3 = Good Health & Well-being. Five P's: People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, Partnership.

questions.stem: Neonatal mortality refers to deaths within:

options.A: The first 7 days of life only

questions.explanation: Neonatal = first 28 days (early <7 days, late 7–<28). Infant mortality = first year; under-5 mortality = first 5 years.

questions.stem: Maternal health, as defined by WHO, covers women's health during:

options.B: Pregnancy, childbirth and the postnatal (postpartum) period

questions.explanation: Maternal health = health during pregnancy, childbirth and the postnatal period.

questions.stem: The most common reported foodborne disease / zoonosis in the EU is caused by:
questions.explanation: Campylobacter (campylobacteriosis) is the most commonly reported zoonosis in the EU (EFSA/ECDC One Health Zoonoses Report).

questions.stem: Most cancer-related deaths worldwide are due to which cancer?
questions.explanation: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death globally (~1.9M deaths in 2021).

questions.stem: The leading cause of death from injury for people aged 5–29 is:
questions.explanation: Road traffic injuries lead for ages 5–29; three of the top five causes of death in this group are injury-related (road traffic, homicide, suicide).

questions.stem: The most common cause of death in highly developed countries is:
questions.explanation: In developed countries, NCDs — led by cardiovascular disease — dominate mortality (injuries typically rank ~3rd after CVD and cancer).

questions.stem: The key difference between an "accident" and an "injury" in modern public health is that:

options.A: They mean exactly the same thing
options.B: Injuries are predictable and preventable, whereas "accident" implies random/unavoidable fate

questions.explanation: Modern public health rejects "accident": injuries follow predictable epidemiological patterns and are preventable (energy transfers that can be blocked).

questions.stem: Communicable vs non-communicable diseases — the defining difference is:

options.A: Communicable can be transmitted from an infected source to a host; non-communicable cannot
options.D: Communicable never have a chronic form

questions.explanation: Communicable = caused by a pathogen and transmissible host-to-host; non-communicable = not transmitted between people. Either can be acute or chronic.

questions.stem: Herd (community) immunity is achieved when:

options.B: Enough of the population is immune to break transmission (R<1)

questions.explanation: Enough immune individuals → the chain of transmission breaks (R<1); 100% coverage is not required and the threshold depends on the agent.

questions.stem: Which quality dimension means providing the same care regardless of age, sex, ethnicity or income?
questions.explanation: Equitable care = same quality regardless of personal characteristics — one of the 7 WHO/IOM quality dimensions.

questions.stem: Healthy ageing (WHO) is defined as:

options.A: The absence of any disease in old age
options.B: Developing and maintaining functional ability that enables wellbeing in older age

questions.explanation: Healthy ageing = maintaining functional ability (intrinsic capacity × environment) for wellbeing — not merely the absence of disease.

questions.stem: A doctor responds to a vaccine-hesitant parent: "It sounds like you're worried about side effects because of something you read." This communication technique is:
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing the patient's stated feeling back to them = reflective listening (a motivational-interviewing skill). Open-ended questions and affirmations (positive feedback) are the related techniques.

questions.stem: "How to stop smoking in a community" is best addressed by:

options.A: Only prescribing nicotine patches to individuals
options.B: Population measures: smoke-free laws, taxation, mass-media campaigns, graphic warnings + cessation support
options.C: Ignoring it until people get sick
options.D: Banning all doctors from discussing it

questions.explanation: Community-level tobacco control combines primordial/primary prevention (smoke-free laws, taxation, marketing bans) with health-education campaigns and cessation support — the Behaviour Change Wheel.

questions.stem: A "community" (definition) can refer to:

options.B: A geographical area OR a group sharing a characteristic (interest or a disease)
options.C: Only people with the same disease

questions.explanation: Community = a geographical area OR a group with a shared characteristic (religion, sport, interest, or a particular disease).

questions.stem: Which best describes health promotion?

options.B: Enabling people to increase control over and improve their health via social & environmental interventions

questions.explanation: Health promotion (Ottawa 1986) enables people to increase control over their health, addressing the root causes of ill health — not just treatment.

questions.stem: The WHO's overall goal for reducing the life-expectancy gap between socioeconomic groups is at least:
questions.explanation: A reduction of at least 25%.
